channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Trump’s $1.8 Billion Fund Tests Constitutional Limits
item.description: The deal the president reached with his own subordinates relies on a mechanism created by Congress that legal experts had warned was subject to manipulation.
item.dc:creator: Adam Liptak
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The addendum to the federal government’s settlement with President Trump flirted with a grave question: Can the president pardon himself?

item.title: Jan. 6 Police Officers Sue to Block Trump’s Payout Fund
item.description: The two officers accused the administration of creating a “slush fund” to reward rioters and groups that committed violence on behalf of President Trump.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston for The New York Times
item.media:description: Officer Daniel Hodges of Washington’s Metropolitan Police, left, and Harry Dunn, then an officer with the U.S. Capitol Police, at a congressional hearing in 2022. The two filed suit on Wednesday.

item.title: Prison to Pardons to Payouts: Jan. 6 Rioters Are Elated at Trump’s $1.8 Billion Fund
item.description: The possibility that people who ransacked the Capitol could get money from the government they attacked is the latest head-spinning twist in President Trump’s effort to rewrite the history of Jan. 6.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston for The New York Times
item.media:description: The pro-Trump mob at the Capitol in 2021. If all of the Jan. 6ers sought money from the fund and received the same amount, the payouts would be around $1.125 million each.

item.title: Cuba Says the Trump Administration Is Not Negotiating in Good Faith
item.description: Cuba’s ambassador to the United Nations said in an interview that Havana wanted to talk but the Trump administration was creating pretexts for military action.
item.dc:creator: Jack Nicas
item.media:credit: Hiroko Masuike/The New York Times
item.media:description: Ernesto Soberón Guzmán, Cuba’s ambassador to the U.N., in New York on Wednesday.

item.title: Rubio, in Video, Urges Cubans to Align With Trump Administration
item.description: In a rare direct address, Secretary of State Marco Rubio blamed the former leader Raúl Castro for the country’s longstanding electricity and resource shortages.
item.dc:creator: Ali Watkins
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Secretary of State Marco Rubio delivered a message directed to the Cuban people.

item.title: Early War Goal Was to Install Hard-Line Former President as Iran’s Leader
item.description: An Israeli strike designed to free Mahmoud Ahmadinejad from house arrest in Tehran, U.S. officials said, was part of an effort to bring about regime change and put him in power.
item.dc:creator: Mark Mazzetti, Julian E. Barnes, Farnaz Fassihi and Ronen Bergman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: Former President Mahmoud Ahmadinejad of Iran registering as a candidate in the presidential election in Tehran in 2024.

item.title: Iran Threatens to Strike Beyond the Middle East if the U.S. Resumes Attacks
item.description: The warning was issued as President Trump and Vice President JD Vance say progress is being made toward a deal, while keeping open the threat of renewed strikes.
item.dc:creator: Euan Ward and Jenny Gross
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: Members of Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps stand near a drone display at a government-organized march in Tehran last month.

item.title: Barney Frank, Gay Pioneer and Liberal Stalwart in Congress, Dies at 86
item.description: Often voted the “brainiest,” “funniest” and “most eloquent” member of the House, he was also the first to come out voluntarily and helped normalize being openly gay in public office.
item.dc:creator: Katharine Q. Seelye
item.category: Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act (2010)

item.title: Former Congressman Barney Frank, Now in Hospice, Offers Democrats Frank Words of Advice
item.description: Mr. Frank speaks about the missteps of the Democratic Party and his hope for its future. “Frankly, if I weren’t dying, people wouldn’t be paying as much attention.”
item.dc:creator: Jenna Russell
item.category: Same-Sex Marriage, Civil Unions and Domestic Partnerships
item.media:credit: Greg Miller for The New York Times
item.media:description: Former Representative Barney Frank in Ogunquit, Maine, in 2017. Mr. Frank, who has congestive heart failure, is speaking out on the future of the Democratic Party.

item.title: Timmy the Whale Got Stranded Off the German Coast. Then Things Got Weird.
item.description: A humpback that became stuck in the Baltic seemed to unite a nation in hope. Rescue efforts followed, and then finger-pointing.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley and Jenny Gross
item.media:credit: Jens Büttner/DPA, via Associated Press
item.media:description: The humpback whale, nicknamed Timmy, trapped near the island of Poel, Germany, in April.

item.title: House Passes Housing Bill, Uniting on a Measure to Bring Down Costs
item.description: The legislation, which had been stalled amid Republican divisions, passed overwhelmingly, signaling an eagerness in both parties to address affordability in an election year.
item.dc:creator: Ronda Kaysen
item.media:credit: Sean Rayford for The New York Times
item.media:description: The first major housing legislation in 36 years passed in the House, 396 to 13, on Wednesday.

item.title: Prosecutor Is Charged With Stealing Sealed Report on Trump Documents Case
item.description: The Justice Department accused Carmen Lineberger of emailing records related to the special counsel’s investigation to a personal account.
item.dc:creator: Chris Cameron
item.category: Federal Criminal Case Against Trump (Documents Case)
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: In the indictment, prosecutors said Carmen Lineberger emailed an internal memorandum and a report — an apparent reference to the report by Jack Smith — to a personal address.

item.title: RFK Jr. Fires Leaders of Key Health Task Force
item.description: The health secretary has undermined the work of the U.S. Preventive Services Task Force over the past year.
item.dc:creator: Maggie Astor
item.media:credit: Demetrius Freeman for The New York Times

item.title: Jeff Bezos Praises Trump’s Second Term as ‘More Mature’
item.description: The Amazon founder and owner of The Washington Post also rejected the idea that he has made changes at his companies to gain favor with the president.
item.dc:creator: Katie Robertson

item.title: OpenAI Prepares to File for an I.P.O. in Coming Weeks
item.description: OpenAI would be one of the most highly anticipated potential initial public offerings, in what is set to be a major year of I.P.O.s for Silicon Valley.
item.dc:creator: Mike Isaac and Cade Metz
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sam Altman, OpenAI’s chief executive. The company is facing challenges from the tech giant Google and an A.I. rival, Anthropic.

item.title: To 2026 Graduates Facing a Bleak Job Market, 1991 Grads Have Some Advice
item.description: The New York Times profiled young job seekers in 1991. Today, their own children are entering a work force in flux.
item.dc:creator: Sydney Ember
item.category: Rutgers, The State University of New Jersey
item.media:credit: via Sharon Dilling; Michelle Gustafson for The New York Times

item.title: Meta Lays Off 8,000 Employees, As A.I. Casualties Mount
item.description: Employees have signed petitions against being tracked by A.I. and were trying to figure out who had been let go on Wednesday, as the Silicon Valley giant tries to transform into an A.I.-first company.
item.dc:creator: Eli Tan, Kalley Huang and Mike Isaac
item.media:credit: Ian C. Bates for The New York Times
item.media:description: Meta’s headquarters in Menlo Park, Calif. The company, which renamed itself for the metaverse, is transforming itself for the era of artificial intelligence.

item.title: Majority of Fed Officials Embraced Possibility of Higher Rates at Latest Meeting
item.description: A record of the April meeting, Jerome H. Powell’s last as chair, underscored the extent to which the war with Iran has upended the economic outlook.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Federal Reserve meeting in April was Jerome H. Powell’s last as chair.

item.title: Trump Gets His Payback in Primary Elections, but It Comes at a Cost in Congress
item.description: Republican senators are angry the president is working to unseat their colleagues. But he is also creating more free agents in his own party in Congress willing to defy him.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: After losing his primary race to a Trump-backed challenger, Senator Bill Cassidy, Republican of Louisiana, voted with Democrats in support of a measure to limit the war in Iran.

item.title: Candidates Backed by Trump, Ocasio-Cortez Triumph in Key House Primaries
item.description: The president’s preferred candidate ousted an incumbent in Kentucky, while a Democratic primary in Pennsylvania was a win for the democratic socialists.
item.dc:creator: Michael Kruse
item.media:credit: Maddie McGarvey for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ed Gallrein, a farmer and former Navy SEAL, defeated Representative Thomas Massie on in the Republican primary race in Kentucky’s Fourth Congressional District.

item.title: All the Republicans Trump Has Taken Down in Midterm Primaries
item.description: The president has spent a great deal of political capital this spring trying to unseat fellow Republicans he sees as disloyal, and he has succeeded.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Medina
item.media:credit: Aj Mast/Associated Press
item.media:description: President Trump’s first conquest this month was helping to defeat five Indiana state lawmakers who had opposed his effort to draw new congressional maps in the state.

item.title: San Diego Mosque Shooting Follows Alarm About Rising Islamophobia
item.description: Muslims pointed to a rise in overt hatred online, political attacks and harassment in the months before the killing of three people outside an Islamic center.
item.dc:creator: Shaila Dewan and Jill Cowan
item.category: Islamic Center of San Diego Shooting (2026)
item.media:credit: John Francis Peters for The New York Times
item.media:description: Families outside the Islamic Center of San Diego where shooters killed three people this week.

item.title: Ebola Outbreak Is Unlikely to Become Global Threat, W.H.O. Says
item.description: Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, the World Health Organization’s director general, said the outbreak in central Africa was “not a pandemic emergency.”
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel, Nick Cumming-Bruce and Ephrat Livni
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: Fabrice Coffrini/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, the director-general of the World Health Organization, speaking in Geneva on Wednesday.

item.title: Ebola Containment Efforts May Have Been Hindered by USAID Shutdown and CDC Cuts
item.description: Aid cuts by the Trump administration have shut down crucial disease surveillance networks and medical supply chains in East Africa.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli and Stephanie Nolen
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
United States Agency for International Development
item.media:credit: Jospin Mwisha/Agence France-Presse via Getty Images
item.media:description: Monitoring visitors in Goma, in the Democratic Republic of Congo, where Ebola cases have been confirmed, on Monday.

item.title: Ebola Crisis Sparks Debate Over Global Health Double Standards
item.description: To some Africans, the claim that the continent’s largest health agency had already bungled its response scratched a familiar wound.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Mpoke Bigg
item.category: Africa Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: Moses Sawasawa/Associated Press
item.media:description: Red Cross workers carry the coffin of a person who died of Ebola at a health center in Rwampara, the Democratic Republic of Congo, on Wednesday.

item.title: James Murdoch Buys Half of Vox Media
item.description: The media scion is buying Vox Media’s podcast network, New York magazine and Vox.com for more than $300 million.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin and Jessica Testa
item.media:credit: Victor Llorente for The New York Times
item.media:description: James Murdoch left, and Jim Bankoff, right.

item.title: Return to the Land, a Whites-Only Community, Is Sued for Discrimination
item.description: Return to the Land, a 160-acre development requiring members be white and heterosexual, is breaking fair housing and civil rights laws, according to a lawsuit.
item.dc:creator: Debra Kamin
item.media:credit: Whitten Sabbatini for The New York Times
item.media:description: The founders of Return to the Land, a whites-only community in Arkansas, say they aren’t breaking any laws. A new lawsuit will test that argument.

item.title: Arkansas Housing Development Restricted to White Residents Sparks Legal Concerns
item.description: Housing rights experts say a community restricted to white residents is illegal, but the creators believe they could win a potential challenge in court in the current political climate.
item.dc:creator: Debra Kamin and Whitten Sabbatini
item.media:description: Eric Orwoll, president of Return to the Land, said his group can reject an applicant “who doesn’t present as white.”

item.title: A Founder of e.l.f. Cosmetics Gave Up the High Life to Be a Catholic Priest
item.description: A mystical encounter 12 years ago led Scott Vincent Borba, a founder of e.l.f. Beauty, on a journey that will culminate this week, when he is ordained as a Roman Catholic priest.
item.dc:creator: Michael Levenson
item.media:credit: Samantha Mejia/Diocese of Fresno
item.media:description: Scott Vincent Borba was ordained as a deacon at the St. Charles Borromeo church in Visalia, Calif., last year.

item.title: Children of Radicals Write to Understand Their Legacies
item.description: Zayd Ayers Dohrn and Harriet Clark on activism, violence, guilt and trying to make sense of their “incomprehensible” early days.
item.dc:creator: John Leland
item.category: Dangerous, Dirty, Violent, and Young: A Fugitive Family in the Revolutionary Underground (Book)
item.media:credit: Sara Messinger for The New York Times
item.media:description: Zayd Ayers Dohrn and Harriet Clark, writers with new books, meeting for the first time in New York.

item.title: Book Review: ‘Dangerous, Dirty, Violent, and Young,’ by Zayd Ayers Dohrn
item.description: Zayd Ayers Dohrn’s parents were leaders of the Weather Underground. His new book traces how their revolutionary ideals collided with their family life.
item.dc:creator: Dana Spiotta
item.category: Dangerous, Dirty, Violent, and Young: A Fugitive Family in the Revolutionary Underground (Book)
item.media:credit: David Handschuh/Associated Press
item.media:description: Zayd Dohrn, age 4, with his parents, Bill Ayers and Bernardine Dohrn, outside federal court in New York in May 1982.

item.title: Trump’s Slush Fund Will Reward Criminals. Americans Will Pay for It.
item.description: The president’s corruption and subversion of democratic tradition risk becoming the norm.
item.dc:creator: The Editorial Board
item.media:credit: Photo Illustration by Philotheus Nisch for The New York Times

item.title: E. Jean Carroll Taught Me to Not Be Afraid
item.description: It’s not easy to make an anti-Trump documentary right now.
item.dc:creator: Ivy Meeropol
item.media:credit: Justine Kurland for The New York Times

item.title: Harvard Caps A’s as Selective Colleges Attack Grade Inflation
item.description: Faculty members overwhelmingly approved a limit on the number of top grades they can give to about a fifth of their undergraduate classes.
item.dc:creator: Mark Arsenault
item.media:credit: Lucy Lu for The New York Times
item.media:description: Harvard University’s campus in Cambridge, Mass.

item.title: Penn Station Renovation Moves Ahead as Developer Is Chosen
item.description: The announcement came a day after Transportation Secretary Sean P. Duffy said the federal government intended to spend $8 billion on the project.
item.dc:creator: Patrick McGeehan
item.media:credit: Todd Heisler/The New York Times
item.media:description: Commuters have long decried Pennsylvania Station in New York City for its dreary corridors.

item.title: Video Shows Itamar Ben-Gvir Taunting Pro-Palestinian Activists
item.description: The national security minister, Itamar Ben-Gvir, posted a video in which he is seen with handcuffed activists forced to kneel on the deck of a ship. It drew a rebuke from Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu.
item.dc:creator: Natan Odenheimer
item.media:credit: Amir Levy/Getty Images
item.media:description: Israel’s national security minister, Itamar Ben Gvir, in the Israeli parliament, on Wednesday.

item.title: After Eurovision Final, Dismay That Politics Didn’t Play a Bigger Role
item.description: Politics isn’t supposed to influence the international song contest, but some people wanted relationships between countries to hold more sway in this year’s voting.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses
item.media:credit: Martin Meissner/Associated Press, via Ap Photo/Martin Meissner
item.media:description: Alexandra Capitanescu representing Romania in the Eurovision Song Contest last week.
